items.title: Claude Sonnet 5 is now default
items.oneliner: Anthropic made Sonnet 5 the default, bringing browser and terminal agents closer to Opus-level performance at lower cost.

items.title: Google shipped cheaper multimodal generation models
items.oneliner: Google launched Nano Banana 2 Lite and Omni Flash, pushing image and video generation toward bulk, latency-sensitive production use.

items.title: Anthropic packaged Claude for scientific research
items.oneliner: Claude Science bundles database search, analysis, and reproducible artifacts, signaling that AI-for-science is becoming a real product category.

items.title: Meta improved non-invasive brain-to-text decoding
items.oneliner: Meta says Brain2Qwerty v2 hit 61% word accuracy overall, making non-invasive neural interfaces look less like science fiction.

items.title: Anthropic regained access to restricted models
items.oneliner: Fable 5 returns globally after export controls lifted, a reminder that frontier model access now depends on geopolitics.

items.title: Google lost a major antitrust damages case
items.oneliner: A Swedish court ordered Google to pay Klarna $1.5 billion, showing antitrust penalties are becoming financially material.

items.title: Godot banned AI-authored code contributions
items.oneliner: Godot will reject AI-written patches because maintainers cannot trust contributors to debug generated code they barely understand.

items.title: OpenAI reportedly found a new cost lever
items.oneliner: OpenAI reportedly found an inference technique that halves costs, which matters more than benchmark wins if usage keeps exploding.

items.title: AWS is embedding AI engineers with customers
items.oneliner: AWS is spending $1 billion on forward-deployed AI engineers, proving enterprise adoption still needs humans beside the models.

items.title: Kubernetes now runs inside the browser
items.oneliner: Ngrok ported core Kubernetes behavior to TypeScript in the browser, making cluster education and demos radically easier to share.
